title: steadyburst-sizing-experiment-notes
summary: - Several mid-market customers (analytics bots, real-time document search, conversational assistants tied to live events) exhibit extremely spiky QPS: quiet 90% of the hour, then 1–5 minute windows of 100–200x baseline. - Traditional reserved pools either over-provision (wasted spend) or rely on very aggressive cold-start tolerances that blow latency SLOs during bursts.
text:
steadyburst-sizing-experiment-notes

Objective:
Run an experiment and capture heuristics for workloads that show long idle periods punctuated by short, high-concurrency bursts ("steadyburst" pattern). Goal is to produce: 1) a sizing template for Dedicated GPU pools, 2) a recommended autoscaling policy (fast ramp + graceful drain), and 3) a short cost estimator step-by-step for customer proposals.

Context / Why this matters:
- Several mid-market customers (analytics bots, real-time document search, conversational assistants tied to live events) exhibit extremely spiky QPS: quiet 90% of the hour, then 1–5 minute windows of 100–200x baseline.
- Traditional reserved pools either over-provision (wasted spend) or rely on very aggressive cold-start tolerances that blow latency SLOs during bursts.

High-level takeaways from experiments (summary):
- Warm-standby small pool + ephemeral burst nodes gives the best cost/latency tradeoff for 70–95% spike duty cycles we saw in tests.
- Prewarm latency is ~30–60ms lower when 1 GPU is kept warm per serving shard vs. no warm nodes.
- Bulk scale-up time (cold node ready for inference) depends on image pull + model load; observed median 45s (min 18s, max 120s) on our staging cluster with 3.5xx GB model (~Llama-2 13B quantized).

Experiment design (quick log):
- Workload generator: lane-based replay of production traffic traces (anonymized). Spike profiles: short (30s), medium (3m), long (15m).
- Pool types tested: 1) baseline single large reserved pool; 2) reserved + prewarm warmers (1/serving shard); 3) reserved + cold-burst with instant routing; 4) hybrid (warmers + burst).
- Metrics collected: p50/p95/p99 latency, cold-start fraction, tokens/sec throughput, GPU utilization, cost per 1k tokens.

Representative numbers (from a single medium spike test):
- Baseline reserved (8xA100-equivalent GPUs): p95 = 420ms, p99 = 950ms, cost/hr = $55 (on commit pricing proxy).
- Warm-standby (2 warm + 6 on-demand burst): p95 = 190ms, p99 = 320ms, cost/hr = $48 (because on-demand spread reduced idle loss).
- Cold-burst only: p95 = 600ms, p99 = 2.6s, cost/hr = $37 but SLA misses unacceptable.

Heuristics & sizing recipe for solutions-eng proposals:
- Step 1: estimate peak concurrent sequences (peak QPS * avg concurrency). Add 10% buffer for KV cache churn.
- Step 2: map sequences/sec to tokens/sec using customer prompt profile (avg prompt length + avg response tokens).
- Step 3: translate tokens/sec to GPU throughput using model profile table (we maintain a small table of tokens/sec per GPU for common models — use conservative numbers for mixed precision).
- Step 4: round up to nearest serving shard size (we use shard sizes of 1, 2, 4 GPUs). Aim to have at least one warm shard per 10% of expected bursts.
- Step 5: add warm-standby nodes equal to max(1, floor(total_shards * 0.15)) for predictable p95 tail.
- Step 6: plan burst headroom as up to 3x baseline reserved depending on contractual SLAs and expected burst length.

Recommended autoscaling policy (fast-ramp + measured drain):
- Trigger: cpu/gpu-utilization-based + request-latency SLO breach fallback.
- Scale-up behavior: aggressive; step scale factor = 2x when p95 > target for >10s OR queued requests > 30. Cooldown = 20s. Max simultaneous scale steps = 3.
- Pre-warm signal: use predictive windowing (if scheduled events or traffic pattern indicates >70% chance of spike in next 60s) — pre-create 50% of expected warmers.
- Scale-down behavior: conservative; remove at most 1 shard every 5m with a final drain window of 90s per shard to avoid inflight truncation. Consider keeping a minimal warm pool for 30m after a major burst.
- Fallbacks: route to smaller quantized model variant automatically if pool exhausted (configured rollout of model-family fallback).

Operational runbook snippets (for POC handoff):
- Pre-POC checklist: confirm KMS access for any encrypted model artifacts, validate container image size (aim <6GB for faster startup where possible), set up test traces and a dedicated alert channel.
- During POC: run three synthetic spike profiles (short/medium/long), capture 2x runs each, and tag traces with node allocation events.
- Post-POC: provide an "SLO risk" table showing expected p99 misses per million requests under three capacity options (conservative, balanced, low-cost).

Cost estimator (simple day-1 calc to include in proposals):
- Inputs: model (profile), expected avg qps, peak qps, avg tokens per req, reserved commit hours per month, burst hours per month.
- Formula (text): monthly_reserved = reserved_gpus * commit_price * committed_hours; burst_cost = sum(burst_gpus * on_demand_price * burst_hours); total = monthly_reserved + burst_cost + small buffer (10% ops).
- Example: 6 reserved A100-equivalent @ $45/hr * 24*30 = $194,400/month (note: use commit discounts normally), burst ~ 4 GPUs * $9/hr * 10h/month = $360. Our job is to show both delta and ratio so customer sees saving by using warm-burst hybrid.

Open questions / follow-ups: 
- Validate image model load times on customer on-prem (air-gapped) environments — we saw +30–90s variability by registry/network setup.
- Confirm whether customer prefers deterministic reserved commit pricing vs. burstable credits model (affects recommendation to keep larger warm pool).
- Need a quick script to convert customer-provided trace CSVs into our lane-based workload format (ticketed as JIRA-10021).

Notes for workshop slides:
- Slide 1: "What is steadyburst?" simple sketch and two customer examples.
- Slide 2: cost vs latency tradeoff heatmap (x=warm pool size, y=burst hours/month).
- Slide 3: recommended autoscale policy and SLO guardrails.
- Slide 4: action items & PoC next steps (data we need, timeline).

Unresolved design trade: how much warm pool is cultural vs technical? Some product teams prefer zero warm nodes for simpler billing — we should prepare a "zero-warm" fallback pattern with explicit latency disclaimers.

Appendix (quick cheat list):
- Conservative tokens/sec per GPU (13B quantized): 1200–1800 t/s depending on sequence length.
- Warm preheat gain: 15–35% lower p50 latency observed.
- Good default p95 SLO for conversational product = 300ms; for batch inference 800ms.

-- end --

title: Clarity Week — Decision Grid and All-Staff Orientation
summary: This doc captures the working decisions, asks, and run-of-show for the upcoming Clarity Week (company-wide alignment and planning). Primary goal: converge on the set of cross-company commitments for the next 90 days and produce a single, actionable OKR slate for exec review + an all-staff orientation that surfaces risks and handoffs.
text:
Clarity Week — Decision Grid and All-Staff Orientation

Purpose:
This doc captures the working decisions, asks, and run-of-show for the upcoming Clarity Week (company-wide alignment and planning). Primary goal: converge on the set of cross-company commitments for the next 90 days and produce a single, actionable OKR slate for exec review + an all-staff orientation that surfaces risks and handoffs.

Audience:
- Company execs (founders, VC partners optional)
- Org leads (ENG, PRODUCT, SRE, SALES, CS)
- People ops / comms

Desired outcomes by EOD Friday:
1) Finalized top-3 company OKRs with metrics and owners.
2) Run-of-show for all-staff orientation + 1-pager for ICs.
3) Decision grid resolved for 6 cross-org tradeoffs (staffing, infra spend, launch scope).
4) List of unblock actions (owner + ETA) to feed into next sprint planning.

Context / constraints (working notes):
- Q2 budget flexibility: modest runway for Dedicated capacity pilots, but hiring must be prioritized vs critical infra spend.
- Customer commitments: two enterprise pilots in May require Private deployment support + SOC2 artifacts.
- Product roadmap: Hosted API feature parity for streaming/tool-calling expected by mid-Q2; Optimize toolkit MVP slated for beta.

Draft company-level OKRs (working — numbers are baseline + targets):
OKR 1 (Reliability & SLA):
- Objective: Deliver consistent latency & uptime for production inference customers.
- KR1: 95th percentile latency (hosted text generation, 1k-token distribution) <= 120ms (baseline 160ms).
- KR2: Platform uptime >= 99.9% for hosted API (baseline 99.7%).
- KR3: Onboard 2 enterprise customers onto Dedicated (baseline 0).
Owner: Riley Chen (eng-sre)

OKR 2 (Unit Economics):
- Objective: Reduce per-token inference cost for typical PLG workloads.
- KR1: Reduce per-token cost by 20% on high-volume routes via batching/caching (baseline $0.0009/token).
- KR2: Deploy two optimized quantization profiles covering 70% of tail traffic.
Owner: Sofia Martinez (apply-ml / optimize)

OKR 3 (Developer Growth / Product):
- Objective: Make Redwood the easiest inference platform for engineering teams to adopt.
- KR1: Improve developer onboarding NPS from 6.0 -> 7.8 (survey baseline 6.0).
- KR2: Reduce median time-to-first-successful-call (new orgs) from 45min -> 20min.
Owner: Miguel Torres (devexp)

Notes: these OKRs are intentionally compact — we may move a couple of KRs under product/dedicated teams depending on readiness. Need CFO signoff on cost-reduction targets.

Decision grid (top-of-mind cross-company tradeoffs — matrix summarized as bullets):
1) Dedicated capacity expansion vs. hiring ML infra engineers
- Decision criteria: customer commitments, time to revenue, one-time infra cost vs OPEX.
- Recommendation: postpone large Dedicated expansion; prioritize two-month contract to satisfy pilot customers + hire 1 infra engineer (contract-to-hire) to stabilize rollout.
- Open question: CFO threshold for commit > $Xk requires exec review (see JIRA RDW-987).

2) Aggressive quantization rollout vs. quality guardrails
- Tradeoff: cost savings vs. potential quality regression for prompt-heavy customers.
- Recommendation: phased rollout with canary on 10% traffic + automatic rollback triggers tied to eval prompt set.

3) All-staff orientation length (90m deep-dive vs 60m high-level)
- Recommendation: 60m company-wide, follow-up discipline-specific deep dives (30m) next week.

4) Feature freeze window during platform migration
- Recommendation: soft freeze on non-critical product launches for two weeks around migration; emergency exceptions allowed via triage ticket.

Run-of-show — All-staff orientation (60 minutes):
- 00:00–05:00: Welcome + housekeeping (CEO)
- 05:00–20:00: Company priorities & top-3 OKRs (Exec sponsor for each gives 3 slides + 2 min)
- 20:00–35:00: Reliability & incident-sharing highlight (SRE: short postmortem + wins)
- 35:00–45:00: Product momentum demo: hosted API streaming + Optimize quick wins
- 45:00–55:00: Commitments & what success looks like (Owners call out first two weeks)
- 55:00–60:00: Live Q&A (moderated, burning questions only)

Prep notes for presenters:
- Slides: max 3 slides per Exec sponsor. Single slide: what we commit, why it matters, how will we measure.
- Submit slides to /confluence/clarity-week-decks by Tue 03/03 EOD. (CONFLUENCE-2345)
- Speakers: mic check 10 min before (Zoom host: Jordan Blake).

Oriented one-pager for ICs (to publish post-allstaff):
- What we decided (top-3 OKRs)
- Immediate asks (list of 1–3 per team)
- How to escalate blockers (triage steps + Slack channel)
- Where to find the artifacts (links)

Roles + owners (quick):
- Program lead / convener: Asha Patel (company-ops) — overall ship timeline, decision synth.
- Run-of-show host / comms: Jordan Blake — logistics, slide collation.
- OKR steward: Miguel Torres — collate KR metrics and dashboards.
- SRE / Reliability owner: Riley Chen — latency / uptime KR runs.
- Evals / quality owner: Sofia Martinez — define prompt sets and regression gates.

Timeline (working):
- Mon 03/01: Leadership finalize draft OKRs (this doc -> exec slack review).
- Tue 03/02: Team leads submit slide drafts.
- Wed 03/03: Dry run with execs (30m) + final slide upload.
- Thu 03/04: All-staff orientation (live).
- Fri 03/05: Publish IC one-pager + OKR tracker in Confluence.

Risks & mitigations (brief):
- Risk: Missing telemetry for KR measurement (some routes not instrumented). Mitigation: Miguel to prioritize event+metric backlog — aim to instrument top 6 routes by Wed.
- Risk: Quality regressions from quantization. Mitigation: Sofia to own canary prompt set and run daily gating tests.
- Risk: Enterprise pilot delays (SOC2 docs). Mitigation: Legal + Solutions Eng to run sweep and provide prioritized checklist; escalate to COO if > 1 week delay.

Open questions (to resolve in exec sync):
- Q1: CFO approval threshold for Dedicated expansion spend — what exact number triggers formal approval? (Action: Asha to get clear by Tue).
- Q2: Are we comfortable with a public-facing developer NPS survey in early Q2 or should it stay internal for another cycle? (Action: Miguel to propose sample cadence).
- Q3: Does the sales team commit to two enterprise pilots landing this quarter? (Action: Sales lead to confirm by Wed).

Unresolved ops items / notes to self:
- Create short feedback form for orientation (post-event) — keep it 3 questions.
- Build visibility dashboard for KR progress (simple table in Confluence).
- Re-check timezone-friendly timing for APAC participants; consider recording.

Next steps (owner + ETA):
- Asha: finalize decision grid + escalate CFO spend threshold (Tue 03/02).
- Miguel: instrument top routes + draft developer NPS cadence (Wed 03/03).
- Riley: run p99 latency deep-dive and draft mitigation steps (Thu 03/04).
- Sofia: prepare canary eval prompt sets and rollout checklist (Wed 03/03).
- Jordan: collect slides, set up Zoom + recording + Q&A moderation (Wed 03/03).

Appendix: links & quick references
- Confluence deck stub: CONFLUENCE-2345
- Triage ticket: JIRA-RDW-987
- PR for dashboard: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/412
- Slack for event coordination: #company-ops (seed reminders 1h / 10m)

Notes about tone and distribution:
- Keep orientation tight and optimistic — highlight measurable wins, be explicit about tradeoffs.
- Avoid overpromising timelines (we will call out contingencies).

Raw brainstorming fragments (keepsake of working doc):
- \"What if\" mini-sessions for teams who want to propose new bets — capture in 2-pager and route to exec review.
- Consider one-off office hours for migrate-to-dedicated questions post orientation.
- Would like a single shared spreadsheet that lists KR owners and status; avoid duplicated trackers.

-- end --

title: Steer policy encoding spec and gradual activation notes
summary: This note defines a compact, versioned encoding for routing/steering policies (steer_policy) used by the control plane. It focuses on: - a canonical, machine-validated schema (YAML-first) for authoring steering rules; - deterministic predicate semantics and normalization rules so authors and the runtime agree; - validation and linting checks to catch unsafe or non-deterministic policies preflight
text:
Steer policy encoding spec and gradual activation notes

Summary:
This note defines a compact, versioned encoding for routing/steering policies (steer_policy) used by the control plane. It focuses on:
- a canonical, machine-validated schema (YAML-first) for authoring steering rules;
- deterministic predicate semantics and normalization rules so authors and the runtime agree;
- validation and linting checks to catch unsafe or non-deterministic policies preflight;
- a safe rollout recipe (monotonic activation, telemetry gates, automatic rollback) that works across multi-region topologies.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals: reproducible evaluation, small surface for human authors, safe progressive activation, simple integration with traffic-compiler.
- Non-goals: full expressive policy language (no Turing-complete scripts), advanced ML-based routing decisions (handled elsewhere), runtime scheduling algorithms.

High-level concepts and rationale:
- steer_policy: top-level object combining a set of named routes, each with matchers, weighting, region/availability constraints, and activation metadata.
- Versioning: schema_version at document root. Runtime supports backward-compatible schema upgrades; any incompatible change requires schema_version bump.
- Deterministic evaluation: predicates are normalized (lowercase keys, trimmed strings, canonical timestamp formats). Hashing for consistent split must use canonical serialization.

Authoring schema (YAML example):

schema_version: v1
policy_name: "search-redirect-steer"
global:
  default_route: "default-model"
  max_canary_pct: 20
  steady_step_pct: 5   # recommended increment for monotonic ramp
routes:
  - id: "v2-llama"
    description: "high-throughput quantized variant"
    matchers:
      - type: prefix
        field: path
        value: "/api/generate"
      - type: header
        field: x-client-tier
        operator: in
        values: ["enterprise","beta"]
    weighting: # weight is a soft override; runtime combines with region affinity
      default: 0
      region_overrides:
        us-west-2: 30
        eu-west-1: 10
    activation:
      enabled: false
      canary:
        initial_pct: 1
        max_pct: 20
        rollout_strategy: monotonic
        metric_gates: ["p95_latency","inference_error_rate"]
    safety:
      abort_on: # if any of these metrics exceed threshold in the canary window
        p95_latency_ms: 250
        inference_error_rate_pct: 1.0
    backoff_policy:
      type: exponential
      base_seconds: 60
      max_seconds: 3600

  - id: "fallback-cpu"
    description: "cpu fallback variant used when GPU pools saturated"
    matchers: []
    weighting:
      default: 70
    activation:
      enabled: true

predicate DSL and normalization rules:
- Primitive matchers: prefix, exact, regex, header, cookie, client_id, region.
- Logical composition: AND across listed matchers. OR is represented by multiple routes with overlapping matchers and disjoint ids.
- Operators: exact, in, prefix, regex. Regex must be PCRE-compatible; disallow back-reference constructs that cause exponential-time match.
- Normalization: header names lowercased; header values trimmed. timestamps and durations normalized to RFC3339 and seconds respectively.
- Canonical serialization for hashing: YAML->sorted-keys JSON->UTF8 bytes. The control plane will use sha256(hex) of canonical JSON for stable bucket assignment.

Validation and linter rules (preflight):
- Schema validation: required fields present, types correct, no undeclared keys.
- Determinism checks: regex complexity estimation, no time-based randomness in predicates.
- Safety checks: if activation.enabled == true and canary.initial_pct > 0 then must have metric_gates at least one stable metric (latency or error).
- Conflict detection: overlapping routes with identical ids or identical matcher sets flagged. Provide a warning when multiple routes match same exact inputs unless intended.
- Weighting sanity: default weights sum must be <=100. Region overrides must reference known regions list.
- Route immutability annotations: authors should mark fields that are immutable without schema_version bump (e.g., id, predicate keys).

Linter examples (sample warnings):
- WARN: regex uses nested quantifiers (may be catastrophic) at routes[0].matchers[1]
- ERROR: region_overrides references unknown region "ap-south-9"
- ERROR: canary.metric_gates empty while canary initial_pct > 0

Rollout mechanics (safe progressive activation):
1) Author submits policy draft; control-plane linter runs and blocks on ERRORs, surfaces WARNs.
2) Preflight dry-run: traffic-compiler simulates incoming request corpus (production sample) and reports match distribution, expected qps per route, and estimated resource footprint.
3) Canaries: if activation.canary.initial_pct > 0, we create a canary bucket using consistent-hash-based bucketing seeded by policy checksum. The canary bucket uses the same affinity keys as production (client_id, session, or user_id) to ensure sticky routing during ramp.
4) Telemetry gates: at each steady_step_pct increment we require passing metric_gates for a sliding window (default 5m evaluation window, min 5k tokens or 1k requests). Gate conditions include p95 latency increase relative to baseline, error rate absolute and delta, and cost-per-token deviation.
5) Backoff and rollback: if safety.abort_on triggers, activation reverts to previous distribution and backoff_policy is applied; if multiple failures occur, policy is disabled and paged to oncall.
6) Finalize: after reaching max_canary_pct and sustained healthy metrics for N windows (configurable; default 6 windows), activation.enabled flips to true and weighting.default/region_overrides take effect.

Multi-region considerations:
- Region-aware overrides should be explicit. If a route does not list region_overrides, control plane assumes global default weighting.
- Affinity: to preserve session stickiness during cross-region failovers, use a two-layer hash: primary key (client_id) hashed to region bucket; secondary hash resolves to route variant. Keep hash seeds stable across deployments.
- Geo-fallback topology: define fallback_order in global section: e.g., [us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1]. In case regional capacity constraint, route selection consults fallback_order and simulated cost distance.
- Partial outages: during regional degradation, canary activation should be paused for that region and resumed for healthy regions only. The preflight simulator includes capacity profiles per region for more accurate resource estimation.

Testing and CI integration:
- Unit tests: authors should include a small set of example inputs and expected route ids. These are validated in PR checks.
- Simulation harness: the control-plane exposes an offline simulator that replays ~24h of sampled production traffic to produce match heatmaps and resource hotspots.
- Canary smoke: when a canary is created, a smoke-runner issues synthetic requests that exercise under-covered paths (e.g., large-payload edge cases) to surface latency regressions faster.

Operational runbook (high level):
- When alerted on canary failure: 1) Pause rollout 2) Check simulation report and sample traces 3) If reproducible, revert policy and open PLAT incident with attached traces 4) If not reproducible, increase eval window and continue slow ramp.
- Rollback mechanics: rollback is immediate change of route checksum for affected policy and traffic-compiler recomputes bucket assignment. Because bucket seeds are checksum-derived, the rollback preserves client affinity where possible.

Checklist for safe promotion:
- [ ] Linter: no ERRORs
- [ ] Preflight simulation: no >10% unexpected CPU jump per region
- [ ] Canary metrics: p95 latency delta < 15% and error rate delta < 0.2% for 6 windows
- [ ] Feature flag gated for emergency kill-switch
- [ ] Runbook updated with expected rollback triggers

Appendix: small JSON canonicalization example (how we compute checksum):
We first produce a deterministic JSON by sorting map keys, normalizing strings, and representing timestamps as RFC3339. Example canonical JSON for route id "v2-llama":
{
  "description": "high-throughput quantized variant",
  "id": "v2-llama",
  "matchers": [
    {"field": "path", "type": "prefix", "value": "/api/generate"},
    {"field": "x-client-tier", "type": "header", "operator": "in", "values": ["enterprise","beta"]}
  ],
  "weighting": {"default": 0, "region_overrides": {"eu-west-1": 10, "us-west-2": 30}}
}

We then compute sha256(hex) of the UTF8 bytes of the canonical JSON and use the hex prefix for bucket seeding.

Open questions (for follow-up):
- Should we allow per-tenant override for default_step_pct? (pros: flexibility; cons: complexity in global rollouts)
- How to express temporal routing rules (e.g., holiday overrides) without granting Turing-complete expressions? Proposal: a timed_activation block with RFC3339 window and explicit rollback policy.
- Standardizing the metric gate language (threshold vs delta vs ratio) — recommend simple threshold + relative delta for initial rollout.

Notes to reviewers:
- Focus review on determinism rules and safety triggers. If you catch a nondeterministic normalization case (e.g., header ordering ambiguity), flag it.
- I will add a small sample policy library (team-maintained) after schema is approved to reduce author errors.

-- Aisha K. Patel (steering/traffic compiler owner)
